---
pagetitle: "Programming with AI"
---

# Programming with AI {layout="title" data-gp-kicker="WORKSHOP / BAMBERG" data-gp-section="Opening"}

<div class="gp-title-above">Workshop / Session 3</div>
<div class="gp-code">PROGRAMMING WITH AI</div>
<div class="gp-title-below">vibe coding, best practices, agentic systems</div>
<div class="gp-accent-bar" data-gp-underline="tag" aria-hidden="true"></div>

::: {.gp-subtitle}
<span>Thursday, March 19, 2026 · 13:00-15:30</span>
:::

::: {.gp-meta-grid}
::: {.gp-block}
<span class="gp-label">Thu</span> March 19, 2026 · 13:00-15:30
:::
::: {.gp-block}
<span class="gp-label">Part</span> Session 3 of 3
:::
::: {.gp-block}
<span class="gp-label">Focus</span> Vibe coding, debugging, tooling, projects
:::
:::



# About Me {layout="bullets"}

* Max Noichl, doctoral candidate in theoretical philosophy at Utrecht University.
* Philosophy of machine learning. [OPEN](#){.opens-modal data-modal-type="iframe" data-modal-url="images/2508.15929v1.pdf"}
* Data-driven methods for philosophy.
* Computational Metaphilosophy [OPEN](#){.opens-modal data-modal-type="iframe" data-modal-url="images/Reason___Intuition.pdf"}
* OpenAlex Mapper [OPEN](#){.opens-modal data-modal-type="iframe" data-modal-url="https://maxnoichl-openalex-mapper.hf.space"}
<!-- * Scientific Model Transfer [OPEN](#){.opens-modal data-modal-type="iframe" data-modal-url="images/model_transfer.pdf"} -->
* Simulations for social epistemology.
* Doing philosophy with AI.


<!-- # Schedule {layout="table" data-gp-kicker="Agenda / 01" data-gp-section="Plan"}

| Day | Time | Session | Focus |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wed, March 18, 2026 | 14:00-16:30 | Introductory Session | Overview of useful AI tools for research work |
| Thu, March 19, 2026 | 09:30-12:00 | Text Analysis | Notebook-based text analysis with AI support |
| Thu, March 19, 2026 | 13:00-15:30 | Programming | Prompting, debugging, and notebook-based coding with AI support | -->


<!-- # Preparation {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Setup / 02" data-gp-section="Plan"}

* Editor with AI support: ChatGPT, Claude or Cursor; VS Code + extension, Codex / Claude Code GUI, etc. -->

# Preparation {layout="half" data-gp-kicker="Setup / 02" data-gp-section="Plan"}

:::: {.columns .gp-two-col .gp-two-col--qr}
::: {.column width="62%"}

* Website: [https://mnoichl.github.io/workshop_AI_bamberg/](https://mnoichl.github.io/workshop_AI_bamberg/)
* Editor with AI support: ChatGPT, Claude or Cursor; VS Code + extension, Codex / Claude Code GUI, etc.

:::
::: {.column width="38%"}

::: {.gp-figure .gp-figure--qr}
<iframe class="gp-qr-embed" src="qrcode.html?url=https%3A%2F%2Fmnoichl.github.io%2Fworkshop_AI_bamberg%2F" title="QR code to workshop website"></iframe>
:::

:::
:::



<!-- # Plan: Programming with AI {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Scope" data-gp-section="Plan"}

* What "vibe coding" captures
* Where coding assistants are genuinely good
* Where they fail in research code
* Tool use, agents, and the command line
* Verification, privacy, backups, and cost limits
* Project vignettes you can finish in about 90 minutes -->
<!-- 

# Programming with AI {layout="divider" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Thu, March 19" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

Vibe coding, debugging, workflows -->


# Part 2: Coding with AI {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Why Now" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* LLMs turned out to be surprisingly good at coding. [ICPC](#){.opens-modal data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="ICPC.png"}
* One of the biggest contemporary use-cases.

# Vibe coding {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Mode" data-gp-section="Session 3"}


* "Vibe coding" - Karpathy [OPEN TWEET](#){.opens-modal data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="images/vibe_coding_kapathy.png"}
* Vibe-coding is about "taste". [OPEN QUOTE](#){.opens-modal data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="https://preview.redd.it/lettingthevibesbeyourguide-v0-7fgejbqocece1.png?auto=webp&s=e747c6c9ceef7012330b2602fe772cfbe441553a"}
* Iterate by steering.
* Powerful for prototypes, but be wary of technical debt.



<!-- # A huge opportunity, and a huge trap {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Stakes" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* AI can make tests, type hints, docstrings, CLIs, packaging, and README files much easier to add.
* It also makes it easier to generate plausible nonsense, wrong abstractions, broken environments, and fake confidence.
* Benchmarks make coding agents look strong.
* Real workflows are more mixed.
* The lesson is not "AI coding works" or "AI coding fails".
* It depends on task, context, codebase familiarity, and verification setup. -->


# Science as amateur software development {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Framing" data-gp-section="Session 3"}


* Science us amateur software development [OPEN VIDEO](#){.opens-modal data-modal-type="iframe" data-modal-url="https://www.youtube.com/embed/8qzVV7eEiaI"}
* Most of as are not trained as software engineers.
* AI can fill some professionalization gaps.
<!-- * Use AI not only to write analysis code, but to improve the workflow around the code. -->


# All those annoying little things {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Hygiene" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* Commits and commit messages.
* Extensive, up-to-date docstrings.
* Virtual environments, managed dependencies, and configuration files.
* Packaging.
* Testing suits. 
* More visualizations: Look at your data from all angles!
* Extensive README files.
* Increase reproducibility and downstream value.
* *This can make science better! (and worse)*


# Agents {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Tool Use" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* A coding assistant becomes much more useful once it can read and change your files.
* We can steer the agent by adding agent.md files. "Never *do* X, always *do* Y,..."
* Agents can maintain these files themselves.
* For scientific work: tell it to fail loudly!
* We can ask them to create plan, and execute after approval.


<!-- # Prompt engineering is over, sort of {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Prompting" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* The era of "magic spell" prompts is mostly over.
* For coding, the best prompts now look like a bug report, a task ticket, a miniature spec, or a test case.
* What helps most: relevant files, constraints, expected output, verification steps, and explicit stop conditions.
* Better than "make no mistakes":
* `run the tests after each meaningful change`
* `if a command fails, show me the failure and stop`
* `do not claim success unless the checks passed` -->


<!-- # Talk to the thing! {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Interface" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* Natural language is now a real programming interface.
* For exploratory tasks, talking or dictating intent can be genuinely efficient.
* It works best when grounded in artifacts: files, diffs, tests, screenshots, and expected outputs.
* Good interaction pattern: say what you want, point at the relevant files, say how success will be checked, ask for the smallest robust change first. -->

<!-- 
# For scientific work: tell it to fail loudly {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Verification" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* Ask the model to stop on failed tests.
* Surface stack traces and preserve stderr.
* List assumptions and uncertainty.
* Avoid silent fallbacks.
* Avoid inventing results.
* In scientific code, a quiet wrong answer is usually worse than a loud failure. -->


<!-- # Counterintuitively, they are lazy {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Steerability" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* Models often do the minimum strongly implied by the prompt and the harness.
* If you want exhaustive behavior, ask for exhaustive behavior.
* If you want it to inspect every file matching a pattern, say so.
* If you want a debugging log, require one.
* A lot of apparent laziness is really under-specification. -->


<!-- # Chatbots, agents {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Systems" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* A chatbot mostly answers turns in a conversation.
* An agent acts toward a goal across multiple steps, usually with tools, state, and stop conditions.
* For coding, the simplest useful agent often looks like: model, repo access, shell or test runner, docs access, a budget, and a clear done condition.
* You usually do not need a baroque multi-agent architecture.
* Simple, composable setups often work best. -->


# Safety & backups {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Safety" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* AI *will* mess things up sooner or later. [DELETED](#){.opens-modal data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="images/Deleted%20_photos.jpeg"}
* Back up your data, where the AI can't find it!
* 3-2-1 rule: 3 copies, 2 locations, 1 offsite.
* Work with branches, make the model use git.

# Set spending limits! {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Cost" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* Set clear spending limits! [AI BILL](#){.opens-modal data-modal-type="image" data-modal-url="images/reddit_AI__bill.png"}
* Notification thresholds.
* AI-companies are sneaky: Often the defaults are not failsafe.
* Keep your API-keys safe!

<!-- # A practical coding workflow for scientists {layout="bullets" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Workflow" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

* Start in chat when the task is fuzzy.
* Move to an IDE or CLI agent when the task touches real files.
* Give the model the traceback, the environment spec, the expected output, and the test command.
* Ask for a minimal reproduction, the smallest robust patch, a regression check, and a commit message.
* Then review the diff like you would review a junior collaborator's work.
* Fast is good. Auditable is better. -->


# Project Ideas {layout="divider" data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Exercises" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

Little projects for today!


# Idea 01: Personal website via GitHub Pages {layout="bullets" .gp-vignette data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Vignette 01" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

::: {.gp-vignette-scroll}
::: {.gp-vignette-section}
<div class="gp-vignette-section-title">Goal</div>

Build and publish a simple personal website on GitHub Pages without hand-writing *any* HTML and CSS.
:::

::: {.gp-vignette-section}
<div class="gp-vignette-section-title">Things to prompt</div>

<ol class="gp-vignette-prompts">
<li>Create a minimal GitHub Pages project for a personal academic website with Home, About, Projects, and Contact. Use static HTML, CSS, and JS only.</li>
<li>If you want a blog – ask the model to tell you about Jekyll & Hugo...</li>
<li>Give the model information. A CV, an old website, a list of publications...</li>
<li>Ask the model to organize the site.</li>
<li>Find some website you like. Screenshot it. Give the screenshot to your model and ask it  to make your website look like that.</li>
<li>Ask about GDPR, cookie-banners, Impressum, etc. Make the model fix that for you.</li>
<li>Let the model walk you through setting up a Github account and publishing your website.</li>
<li>Iterate! Make it look better!</li>
</ol>
:::

:::


# Vignette 02: Build a small text corpus through web scraping {layout="bullets" .gp-vignette data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Vignette 02" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

::: {.gp-vignette-scroll}
::: {.gp-vignette-section}
<div class="gp-vignette-section-title">Goal</div>

Continue the earlier text-analysis work on your own scraped data from the web: for example a forum, a news archive, a public corpus,...
:::


::: {.gp-vignette-section}
<div class="gp-vignette-section-title">Things to prompt</div>

<ol class="gp-vignette-prompts">
<li>Use the model to research potential data-sources for what you are interested in.</li>
<li>Develop a scraping strategy with the model.</li>
<li>Check robots.txt. Be a good citizen of the internet and scrape respectfully.</li>
<li>Figure out a structured data-format for your results.</li>
<li>Audit for problems.</li>
<li>Figure out the text extraction: HTML to plaintext.</li>
<li>Bring it into the right format for text analysis.</li>
</ol>
:::

:::


# Vignette 03: Write a .qmd presentation and publish it on GitHub Pages {layout="bullets" .gp-vignette data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Vignette 03" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

::: {.gp-vignette-scroll}

::: {.gp-vignette-section}
<div class="gp-vignette-section-title">Goal</div>

Produce a talk deck in Quarto Markdown, render it to Reveal.js, and publish it on GitHub Pages.
:::


::: {.gp-vignette-section}
<div class="gp-vignette-section-title">Things to prompt</div>

<ol class="gp-vignette-prompts">
<li>Start with a talk you already gave, and move it to markdown.</li>
<li>Let the model install quarto.</li>
<li>Make the model turn your .md into a full qmd presentation.</li>
<li>Render locally, and work on your aesthetics.</li>
<li>Figure out figures, bibtex, etc.</li>
<li>Set up github, a repository, and then publish it on GitHub Pages.</li>
<li>Publish it on GitHub Pages.</li>
<li>Learn about github actions to build automatically online!</li>
</ol>
:::

:::


# Vignette 04: Champions League {layout="bullets" .gp-vignette data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Vignette 04" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

::: {.gp-vignette-scroll}
::: {.gp-vignette-section}
<div class="gp-vignette-section-title">Goal</div>

Use the Kaggle <a href="https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/davidcariboo/player-scores/data"><code>davidcariboo/player-scores</code></a> dataset to try out AI assissted coding for sports analytics
:::


::: {.gp-vignette-section}
<div class="gp-vignette-section-title">Things to prompt</div>

<ol class="gp-vignette-prompts">
<li>Have the LLM download the Kaggle dataset <a href="https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/davidcariboo/player-scores/data"><code>davidcariboo/player-scores</code></a> into a local data directory.</li>
<li>Explore! create some figures and tables in `.ipynb` or `.Rmd` to get a feeling for the data.</li>
<li>Develop a modelling strategy with the LLM.</li>
<li>Build the first version of a model, validate on hold out data.</li>
<li>Iterate. Find problems with the model, improve it, and validate again. Try out new model types, machine-learning approaches, etc.</li>
<li>Probe the LLM to find problems with your approach and suggest improvements.</li>
</ol>
:::

:::

# Idea 05: Do your own thing! {layout="bullets" .gp-vignette data-gp-kicker="Session 3 / Vignette 01" data-gp-section="Session 3"}

::: {.gp-vignette-scroll}
::: {.gp-vignette-section}
<div class="gp-vignette-section-title">Goal</div>

Well. it's your own thing!
:::


::: {.gp-vignette-section}
<div class="gp-vignette-section-title">Things to prompt</div>

<ol class="gp-vignette-prompts">
<li> Tell the model what you want to do, and let it help you!</li>
</ol>
:::

:::
